key_differences: with_skill uses structured emoji/box format; without uses plain markdown
with_skill provides [1]-[4] numbered options; without has no selection mechanism
with_skill reports architecture chain validation; without only lists file path
Both correctly read .aria/state-checks.yaml and executed checks
with_skill uses emoji status indicators (✅/⚠️/🔴); without uses table format
with_skill follows exact SKILL.md output spec; without invents own format
Qualitative: with_skill output is more consistent with Aria design system
with_skill uses [1]-[4] numbered options; without uses lettered A/B/C/D format
with_skill references RECOMMENDATION_RULES priority numbers; without uses ad-hoc ordering
with_skill includes confidence percentages; without estimates effort in hours
Both provide actionable recommendations with specific commands

analyst_observations: Eval 2 (custom-health-checks) shows EQUAL pass rate — both agents successfully read and executed .aria/state-checks.yaml. The Skill adds format consistency but not functional advantage here. This is expected: the YAML config is self-describing, so agents can interpret it without guidance.

The strongest signal is eval 1 and 3 where the Skill enforces the [1]-[4] numbered options pattern and priority-based ordering — without the Skill, agents default to their own format choices, breaking UX consistency.

No assertions were non-discriminating (always pass regardless of skill). The custom checks assertions in eval 2 are closest to non-discriminating since both agents passed, but the qualitative difference in format consistency still justifies the Skill.
